name: error-logger
description: Structured JSON logging with correlation IDs for multi-service systems. Use when implementing logging, debugging failures, or tracing errors across services. Triggers on: add logging, error handling, debug failures, trace errors.
capabilities: Create structured JSON log entries with automatic correlation ID generation
Propagate correlation IDs across HTTP requests via X-Correlation-ID headers
Track operations across multiple services with prefix-based correlation IDs
Log errors with context including transaction hashes and failure details
author: BarisSozen
securitySummary: All static findings evaluated as false positives. SHA256 hashes flagged as weak crypto are secure cryptographic values in metadata. Backtick references in documentation are markdown formatting, not shell execution. This is a legitimate logging library skill with no malicious code or operations.
valueStatement: Debugging distributed systems is challenging without correlated logs. This skill provides structured JSON logging with automatic correlation ID propagation across services, making it easier to trace requests through complex microservice architectures.
limitations: Does not provide log aggregation or storage - only generates structured log output
Requires integration with a logging framework to persist logs
Correlation ID format is predefined with specific prefixes (liq, arb, quo, op)
Does not include log visualization or analysis capabilities

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers working with distributed systems
useCases.title: Debug microservice failures
useCases.description: Trace a request as it flows through multiple services by correlating logs with shared IDs. Identify which service introduced a failure and at what point.

useCases.targetUser: DevOps engineers and system reliability engineers
useCases.title: Monitor transaction processing
useCases.description: Track financial or data transactions across services using correlation IDs. Log each step with context for later analysis and auditing.

useCases.targetUser: Site reliability engineers and support engineers
useCases.title: Investigate production incidents
useCases.description: Quickly locate all related log entries for a failed request by correlation ID. Reduce mean time to diagnosis during production incidents.

promptTemplates.title: Basic error logging
promptTemplates.prompt: Add error logging to function [FUNCTION_NAME] using the error-logger skill. Include correlation ID tracking and log the error with context.
promptTemplates.scenario: Adding structured error logging to a single function

promptTemplates.title: HTTP request correlation
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement correlation ID propagation for HTTP requests. Extract X-Correlation-ID from incoming requests and attach it to outgoing requests.
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up distributed tracing across HTTP services

promptTemplates.title: Transaction tracking
promptTemplates.description: Track a financial transaction through multiple services with correlation IDs. Log each step including success and failure states.
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement transaction tracking for [TRANSACTION_TYPE] using the error-logger skill. Use the appropriate correlation ID prefix and log each service interaction.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-service debugging
promptTemplates.prompt: Debug a cross-service request flow. Identify where failures occur by correlating logs across services and extracting the correlation chain.
promptTemplates.scenario: Investigating failures that span multiple microservices

bestPractices: Always propagate correlation IDs to downstream services to maintain trace continuity
Include sufficient context in log entries to enable debugging without requiring log lookup
Use the appropriate correlation ID prefix (liq, arb, quo, op) for the operation type
antiPatterns: Generating new correlation IDs for each service instead of propagating the existing one
Logging errors without including context such as transaction IDs or user identifiers
Using correlation IDs that do not include timestamps for time-based analysis

faq.question: What is a correlation ID?
faq.answer: A correlation ID is a unique identifier attached to a request as it flows through multiple services. It enables tracing the entire request path by linking all related log entries together.

faq.question: How are correlation IDs generated?
faq.answer: Correlation IDs follow the format {prefix}_{timestamp_hex}_{random}. The prefix indicates the operation type (liq, arb, quo, or op) and the remaining parts provide uniqueness and ordering.

faq.question: Do I need to create a new correlation ID for each service?
faq.answer: No. You should propagate the existing correlation ID to downstream services rather than generating new ones. This maintains the trace across the entire request path.

faq.question: What headers are used for correlation ID propagation?
faq.answer: The X-Correlation-ID HTTP header is used to propagate correlation IDs between services. Extract this header from incoming requests and include it in outgoing requests.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without a distributed system?
faq.answer: Yes. Even in monolithic applications, correlation IDs help group related log entries and simplify debugging complex request flows.

faq.question: Does this skill store logs?
faq.answer: No. This skill generates structured log output in JSON format. You need to integrate it with your existing logging framework to persist and view the logs.
